title: What Do You Think Gendo Said To Ritsuko
display_title: What Do You Think Gendo Said To Ritsuko
wikitext:
[]: What Do You Think Gendo Said To Ritsuko. Post by armoredcorecc » Sat Aug 22, 2009 7:45 am Ok, this is my first post (I'm a noob and proud of it!). Anyways, what do you think Gendo said to Ritsuko right before he shot her. It was muted purposely, so no one knows exactly, but I want to know what you thought. Anno is a sneaky guy the way he muted it. Hmmmm Oh the link is pretty funny, and related totally- http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=yzhKiuzUAAE&feature=channel_page

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Aug 22, 2009 8:09 am There's plenty of pre-existing threads about this. In before lock: Here was my theory: I wrote: We know that Anno uses recurring motifs in NGE all the time - in the dialogue, in the visuals, pretty much everywhere. So I kept thinking that Gendo's last line has to be hinted at somewhere in the show. Low and behold, the second time around what do I see? Ep. 21 when Ritsuko first comes to NERV. After Yui's fateful (kinda) encounter with Shogouki we see Naoko, frustrated, talking to Gendo with Ritsuko watching in the background. The conversation goes: Naoko: Is it true? Gendo: Yes, I have no regrets concerning my work. Naoko: Liar! You just can't forget about Yui, can you? Remember Ritsuko talking about hating her mother? I think her affection for Gendo started long before she joined NERV and when she found out her mother was involved, she wasn't a happy camper. Some other ideas pertaining to my theory is that: 1. We know Ritsuko DIDN'T want to end up like her mother. Yet she had fallen for the same guy, and been (or, atleast felt she had been) betrayed by him. 2. I've heard it said that Gendo resembles Anno somewhat. I don't think this is a coincidence because I've often felt Gendo's "evil mastermind with a longing to see his dead wife" came from a very heartfelt place. It's hard to make someone be such a cold bastard yet seem so worth pitying, even feeling sorry for, in EoE where he finally gets to see Yui again. I think that perhaps (although it's just me guessing) that Gendo might be Anno's psychological portrayal of himself. If I'm right, that would make the final line also important pertaining to Anno's thoughts and feelings (perhaps the reason he silenced the line, because it had a deeper meaning to him). 3. The voice actor's statement about the line: "I looked for a way to accept her death at the hands of Ikari. This made the interpretation of "Liar" very important. But the voice-over grew nearer and nearer.... Director Anno must have noticed how I felt. When it came time to do the voice-over, he showed me a single, hidden hint at the last moment. With that one incredible hint, I, and Ritsuko Akagi, were utterly defeated. It hardly needs saying, but Director Anno is incredible. Truly awesome -- a genius." Something that totally defeated her. Noticed Anno showed her a single hidden hint. If that hint was that scene, I think it makes perfect sense. So the line is: 'Ritsuko, I truly' "have no regrets concerning my work." Think how this totally syncs up with everything. Ritsuko seeing the same line fed to her as her mother, and realizing the same fate will befall her in the end, that she couldn't escape the mother she hated's shadow. How it pertains to Gendo's feelings about using people to acheive his goals, even though it's cost him everything. Then notice how it pertains to Anno's feelings. He has no regrets concerning his work. If Gendo represents Anno, then that line could be one of those half truths. He's proud of his work, but saddened by what it's cost him, hence Ritsuko's and Naoko's "Liar".

[]: Post by thewayneiac » Sat Aug 22, 2009 8:36 am ^The problem with this is that the context is all wrong. In the Ep. 21 scene, Naoko is directly challenging Gendo about his feelings, and he is manifestly lying when he says that he doesn't regret his work when that work is what took Yui away from him. He is telling this lie to keep her on his side until she is no longer useful. But, in EOE, why would it occur to Gendo to refer to a decade old conversation, especially when there is no sign he even knew that Ritsuko witnessed it? What would he have been trying to say, and why woud Ritsuko react the way she did? Why would something she's known all along is a lie "utterly defeat" her? What ever he said must have had some meaningful application to her relationship with Gendo. Ritsuko would have treated "I have no regrets concerning my work" as refering to his turning her over to Seele's interrogation . She would not, in that case, have called him a "liar" because she knows he doesn't regret it. Therefore it would make more sense if he said "I truly do regret what I did to You". That would be a bitter lie that would destroy Ritsuko.

[]: Post by Eva Yojimbo » Sat Aug 22, 2009 8:57 am thewayneiac wrote: But, in EOE, why would it occur to Gendo to refer to a decade old conversation, especially when there is no sign he even knew that Ritsuko witnessed it?

[]: Post by Evangelionfreak » Sat Aug 22, 2009 6:00 pm Ritsuko Akagi the truth is, your dye job sucks.

[]: Post by Holy Diver » Sat Aug 22, 2009 6:07 pm I love you.

[]: Post by oOoOoOo » Sat Aug 22, 2009 7:29 pm I agree with the Most Serene and Holy Diver. That's what I'd say before I killed a lover. I'm good like that. And if that isn't enough for you, try these fourteen massive pages of total deliciousness .

 the context is all wrong. In the Ep. 21 scene, Naoko is directly challenging Gendo about his feelings, and he is manifestly lying when he says that he doesn't regret his work when that work is what took Yui away from him. He is telling this lie to keep her on his side until she is no longer useful. But, in EOE, why would it occur to Gendo to refer to a decade old conversation, especially when there is no sign he even knew that Ritsuko witnessed it? What would he have been trying to say, and why woud Ritsuko react the way she did? Why would something she's known all along is a lie "utterly defeat" her? What ever he said must have had some meaningful application to her relationship with Gendo. Ritsuko would have treated "I have no regrets concerning my work" as refering to his turning her over to Seele's interrogation . She would not, in that case, have called him a "liar" because she knows he doesn't regret it. Therefore it would make more sense if he said "I truly do regret what I did to You". That would be a bitter lie that would destroy Ritsuko.
